title: Microbiome kits comparison
text:
Preface
This is a Quarto book.
To learn more about Quarto books visit https://quarto.org/docs/books.

1 + 1

[1] 2

title: 1  Introduction
text:
1.1 Objective
Advances in high-throughput sequencing technologies have revolutionized the study of microbial communities, enabling culture-independent characterization of microbiomes across diverse environments. In particular, 16S rRNA gene sequencing and metagenomics have become widely used to explore microbial diversity, composition, and ecological function (Weinroth et al., 2022). These approaches have significantly expanded our ability to detect previously uncultivable microorganisms and to investigate complex host–microbiome and environment–microbiome interactions.
Despite these advances, microbiome analyses remain highly sensitive to methodological variation. Multiple steps in the workflow—including sampling, storage, DNA extraction, amplification, and sequencing—can introduce biases that affect downstream results . Among these, DNA extraction is one of the most critical sources of variability, as it directly determines DNA yield, quality, and the representation of microbial taxa. Different extraction protocols have been shown to produce significant differences in microbial composition and diversity, even when applied to identical samples (Kennedy et al., 2014).
These discrepancies are largely driven by differences in cell lysis efficiency, DNA recovery, and the presence of inhibitory compounds. For example, microorganisms with robust cell walls, such as Gram-positive bacteria, may be underrepresented when lysis is insufficient, whereas co-extracted inhibitors can negatively affect PCR amplification and sequencing efficiency. As a result, extraction methods can influence not only taxonomic profiles but also ecological interpretations derived from microbiome data (Pu et al., 2025).
Importantly, the magnitude of these biases can vary depending on the type of sample matrix. While many studies have focused on well-characterized matrices such as soil, sediments, or human-associated samples, less attention has been given to more complex or unconventional substrates. This lack of standardization complicates comparisons across studies and limits reproducibility, particularly when different extraction methods are used.
One such challenging matrix is marine skin mucus, which represents a dynamic interface between the host organism and its surrounding environment. This mucus layer plays a key role in host protection, microbial interactions, and environmental sensing, but also presents significant technical challenges for microbiome studies. Its composition—rich in mucopolysaccharides, salts, and host-derived compounds—can interfere with DNA extraction efficiency and downstream enzymatic reactions.
Furthermore, microbiomes associated with marine organisms such as turbot (Scophthalmus maximus) and octopus remain relatively underexplored, particularly in the context of methodological optimization. Most available DNA extraction protocols have been developed for other sample types and may not be directly suitable for mucus-rich marine samples. As a consequence, there is a need to evaluate how these methods perform in such matrices and whether they provide reliable and representative microbiome profiles.
Taken together, these considerations highlight the importance of systematically evaluating DNA extraction methods in non-standard, inhibitor-rich biological matrices, in order to improve reproducibility and ensure accurate microbiome characterization.
The main objective of this study is to compare the performance of different DNA extraction kits for microbiome analysis in marine mucus samples.
Specifically, this study aims to:

title: 1  Introduction
text:
Assess differences in DNA yield, quality, and purity across extraction methods.
Evaluate the impact of extraction protocols on sequencing performance and data quality.
Compare resulting microbial diversity and community composition, identifying potential biases.
Identify the extraction method that provides the most reliable and representative microbiome profiles for mucus samples from marine organisms such as turbot and octopus.

title: 2  Experimental design
text:
Samples of two different species were collected: common octopus (Octopus vulgaris) and turbot (Scophthalmus maximus). Se muestrearon 5 individuos de cada especie. De cada individuo se



Experimental workflow comparing four DNA extraction kits (MasterPure, PureLink, PowerSoil, PowerFecal) across microbiome samples from octopus (Octopus vulgaris) and turbot (Scophthalmus maximus). Each individual (n = 5 per species) was sampled for four subsamples per tissue (skin/mantle or skin/gut), one for each extraction kit, totaling 80 samples.

title: 3  DNA extraction and quantification
text:
3.1 Extraction kits
Four commercially available DNA extraction kits were selected to evaluate their performance across different biological matrices, including marine host-associated samples:

title: 3  DNA extraction and quantification
text:
MasterPureTM Complete DNA and RNA Purification Kit (# MC85200, Epicentre) and Pathogen Lysis Tubes S (50) (# 19091 Qiagen)
QIAamp® PowerFecal® Pro DNA Kit (# 51804, Qiagen) = PowerFecal kit
The first step of the protocol is mechano-chemical cell disruption through bead beating in lysis buffer. The Inhibitor Removal Technology is then used to remove common substances that interfere with downstream PCR applications. DNA from supernatant is captured on MB Spin Column, washed two times and eluted from the silica membrane.
DNeasy® PowerSoil® Pro Kit (# 47014, Qiagen) = PowerSoil kit
The protocol starts with mechano-chemical cell disruption through beat beating in lysis solution. In this kit, Inhibitor Removal Technology is also applied, which should help in removal of organic and inorganic materials such as humic acids, cell debris, and proteins that could interfere with downstream PCR applications. After that, the sample is loaded onto MB Spin Column with silica membrane. DNA is then washed two times and eluted from the silica membrane.
PureLink Genomic DNA Mini Kit (# K182002, Invitrogen) = PureLink kit
Multiple protocols are proposed for this kit. In this case we applied the Gram Positive Bacterial Cell Lysate Protocol for all types of samples. In this protocol, the microorganisms are lysed by a combination of heat and enzymatic disruption (lysozyme and proteinase K). After that, the sample is treated with a cleanup buffer to eliminate PCR inhibitors. The sample is then applied to a PureLink™ spin column. DNA is washed one time and then eluted from the silica membrane.

title: 3  DNA extraction and quantification
text:
3.2 DNA quantification
DNA concentration and quality were assessed using two complementary approaches:

Nanodrop spectrophotometry, providing estimates of DNA concentration as well as purity ratios (A260/A280 and A260/A230)
Qubit dsDNA High Sensitivity Assay (ThermoFisher), providing fluorometric quantification of double-stranded DNA

While Nanodrop measurements were available for all samples, Qubit measurements were only available for a subset of samples. In some cases, Qubit values were reported as out of range, indicating DNA concentrations below the detection threshold.

title: 3  DNA extraction and quantification
text:
4.1 Overview of the dataset
The dataset comprises DNA quantification measurements obtained from two species (Octopus and Turbot), including two tissue types per species. Each biological replicate was processed using all extraction kits, enabling paired comparisons across methods.







Note: Each biological replicate was processed with all extraction kits. Swabbing corresponds to technical replicates within each biological sample.

title: 3  DNA extraction and quantification
text:
4.3 DNA purity

4.3.1 A260/A280 ratio









Lower A260/A230 ratios were frequently observed, particularly in mucus-rich samples. This suggests the presence of residual contaminants such as salts or organic compounds, highlighting the importance of efficient inhibitor removal.

title: 3  DNA extraction and quantification
text:
4.4 Qubit measurements









Qubit measurements were not available for all samples, and a significant proportion of samples fell below the detection threshold. This limits the use of Qubit data as a primary metric for comparison.









Among measurable samples, Qubit-based concentrations broadly followed trends observed with Nanodrop, although differences in sensitivity must be considered

title: 3  DNA extraction and quantification
text:
4.6 Overall, the results demonstrate that:
DNA extraction performance is strongly dependent on both the extraction method and the biological matrix. Mechanical lysis-based kits tend to increase variability but may improve DNA recovery in complex samples. Enzymatic extraction provides more consistent results but may be less efficient in challenging matrices. Inhibitor removal efficiency is a critical factor, particularly for mucus-rich samples such as skin.
Taken together, these findings highlight the importance of selecting an extraction method tailored to the sample type in order to ensure reliable and reproducible microbiome profiling.

title: 4  Summary
text:
In summary, this book has no content whatsoever.
